items.q: What is the difference between a theme and the plot?
items.correct: The plot is what happens; the theme is the underlying idea the story explores.
items.misconception: They are exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: The theme is just the book's title.
items.why: Plot is the sequence of events; theme is the deeper idea (e.g. ambition) those events develop.

items.q: Should you assume the narrator always tells the truth?
items.correct: No - a narrator can be unreliable, so consider their perspective and limits.
items.misconception: The narrator always states the author's exact views.
items.why: Narrative voice shapes what we know; an unreliable narrator may mislead, so trust must be questioned.

items.q: Is prose the same as poetry?
items.correct: No - prose uses continuous sentences and paragraphs rather than verse lines.
items.misconception: Prose and poetry are identical forms.
items.why: Prose is the ordinary form of novels and stories, distinct from the patterned lines of poetry.
The answer examiners want is: In continuous sentences and paragraphs. “In rhyming verse” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's 'I' to tell the story. “'He' and 'she' throughout” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.misconception: Sets the price of a book
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Creates and develops characters. “Sets the price of a book” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A theme in a novel is best described as:
items.correct: A central idea explored across the text
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A central idea explored across the text. “The cover design” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
